title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Focaccia Genovese Recipe: Rosemary & Olive Oil Perfection
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary & olive oil. This easy recipe brings the taste of Italy to your kitchen with a perfect crust & fluffy interior. Get baking!
longTailKeywords: Focaccia Genovese rosemary olive oil recipe
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary and Olive Oil
h2Headings: Tips for Perfect Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia
seo.imageAlt: Close-up of freshly baked Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil, golden crust and dimples
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a slice of Italy with this incredible Focaccia Genovese, featuring fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil.

description: Experience the true taste of Liguria with this authentic Focaccia Genovese, a beloved Italian flatbread. Its golden, dimpled crust hides a soft, airy interior, perfectly infused with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil. Ideal as an appetizer, side, or simply enjoyed on its own.

ingredients.notes: plus extra for greasing and topping

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active. Add 2 tablespoons of the olive oil to the mixture.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the strong bread flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Mix with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Transfer the dough to a lightly oiled surface and knead for 10-15 minutes until it is smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. Alternatively, use a stand mixer with a dough hook for 8-10 minutes.

instructions.title: First Proof
instructions.text: Lightly oil a clean bowl. Place the dough in the bowl, turn it once to coat, then cover with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let the dough rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until it has doubled in size.

instructions.title: Shape the Focaccia
instructions.text: Gently punch down the risen dough and transfer it to a well-oiled 30x40 cm (12x16 inch) baking pan. Using your fingertips, gently stretch the dough to fill the pan, being careful not to tear it. Drizzle generously with more olive oil.

instructions.title: Second Proof and Dimpling
instructions.text: Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap and let the focaccia rise again for 30-45 minutes in a warm place. After the second rise, generously dimple the dough all over with your oiled fingertips, pressing down to create characteristic indentations. Pour a tablespoon or two of olive oil into these dimples, then sprinkle with chopped rosemary and flaky sea salt.

instructions.title: Bake to Perfection
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F). Bake the focaccia for 25-35 minutes, or until it is golden brown and cooked through. The crust should be crisp and the interior soft. Once baked, remove from the oven and transfer to a wire rack to cool slightly before slicing and serving.

tips: For an extra airy focaccia, ensure your dough is quite wet. Don't be afraid if it feels sticky during kneading; a high hydration dough leads to a lighter crumb.
Always use high-quality extra virgin olive oil. Its flavor is central to authentic Focaccia Genovese. Drizzle generously both in the dough and on top before baking.
When dimpling, press your fingers firmly but gently into the dough, almost to the bottom of the pan, to create those characteristic pockets that hold the oil and seasonings.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the sun-drenched Ligurian coast of Italy, where one of the most beloved and iconic breads originates: Focaccia Genovese. This isn't just any focaccia; it's a masterpiece of Italian baking, celebrated for its uniquely crisp crust, incredibly tender and airy interior, and the irresistible aroma of fresh rosemary and high-quality olive oil. More than just a bread, Focaccia Genovese is a symbol of simple elegance and the profound joy found in authentic Italian ingredients and time-honored techniques. Its generous dousing of extra virgin olive oil creates a golden, almost fried bottom crust, while the signature dimples hold pools of oil and coarse sea salt, delivering bursts of flavor with every bite. Whether enjoyed as a snack, an accompaniment, or the base for a delicious sandwich, this rosemary-infused delight promises a taste of Genoa in your own home.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?
Focaccia Genovese, or "fugassa" in the local Ligurian dialect, is a flatbread from Genoa, the capital of the Liguria region in northwest Italy. Unlike other focaccia styles that might be thicker or topped with an array of vegetables, the authentic Focaccia Genovese is characterized by its relatively thin profile (compared to other focaccias), its incredibly airy and open crumb structure, and a distinctively golden, often slightly oily bottom crust. The magic lies in a high-hydration dough, copious amounts of premium extra virgin olive oil, and a generous sprinkling of coarse sea salt, often accompanied by fresh rosemary. Its surface is adorned with characteristic dimples, pressed into the dough before baking, which serve as little reservoirs for the olive oil and salt, ensuring every piece is infused with their rich flavors. It's designed to be a simple, yet profoundly satisfying bread, perfect for breakfast with coffee, as a mid-day snack, or accompanying a meal.

## Ingredients for Authentic Focaccia Genovese
Crafting a truly authentic Focaccia Genovese requires attention to detail and, crucially, high-quality ingredients. The beauty of this bread lies in its simplicity, meaning each component plays a vital role.

*   **Strong Bread Flour (Type 00 or All-Purpose with high protein):** A good quality flour is essential for developing the gluten structure needed for that characteristic airy crumb. Type 00 flour is ideal if you can find it, otherwise, a good quality unbleached all-purpose flour with a protein content of 12-14% will work.
*   **Water:** Lukewarm water helps activate the yeast and contributes to the dough's high hydration, which is key for a tender interior.
*   **Active Dry Yeast or Instant Yeast:** The leavening agent that gives the focaccia its rise and airy texture.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** Incorporated into the dough for flavor.
*   **Sugar (optional):** A pinch of sugar can help "wake up" the yeast, though it's not strictly necessary if your yeast is fresh.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO):** This is perhaps the most critical ingredient. Use a high-quality, fruity EVOO, not just for topping but also generously mixed into the dough and drizzled during the final proofing. It imparts flavor, tenderness, and creates that signature crispy bottom crust.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** Sprigs of fresh rosemary, roughly chopped, infuse the focaccia with its aromatic, slightly piney notes.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** For sprinkling on top, providing a delightful textural contrast and bursts of savory flavor.

## Step-by-Step Focaccia Genovese Recipe

This recipe yields a glorious Focaccia Genovese, perfect for sharing.

**Yields:** 1 large focaccia (approx. 9x13 inch or 10x15 inch pan)
**Prep time:** 30 minutes + 3-4 hours proofing
**Bake time:** 20-25 minutes

**Ingredients:**
*   400g strong bread flour (Type 00 or high-protein all-purpose)
*   320ml lukewarm water (around 35-40°C / 95-105°F)
*   7g active dry yeast (or 5g instant yeast)
*   10g fine sea salt
*   1 tbsp sugar (optional, for active dry yeast)
*   80ml high-quality extra virgin olive oil, plus more for the pan and drizzling
*   2-3 sprigs fresh rosemary, leaves picked and roughly chopped
*   1-2 tsp coarse sea salt, for topping

**Instructions:**

1.  **Activate Yeast (if using active dry):** In a large bowl, combine the lukewarm water, sugar (if using), and active dry yeast. Stir gently and let sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy. If using instant yeast, you can mix it directly with the flour.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour, fine sea salt, and 2 tablespoons (30ml) of the extra virgin olive oil to the yeast mixture. Mix with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms.
3.  **Knead (or No-Knead Method):**
    *   **For a stand mixer:** Attach the dough hook and knead on low speed for 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and pulls away from the sides of the bowl.
    *   **By hand:** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface. Knead for 10-15 minutes, pushing and folding the dough until it's smooth and elastic. It will be a very wet and sticky dough – resist the urge to add too much extra flour.
    *   **No-Knead Option:** If you prefer, after mixing, simply cover the bowl and let it rest for 15 minutes, then perform a series of "stretch and folds" every 15-20 minutes for about an hour (3-4 sets). This develops gluten without traditional kneading.
4.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Lightly oil a large bowl. Transfer the dough to the bowl, turning it once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Generously coat a 9x13 or 10x15 inch baking pan with 2-3 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil, ensuring the sides are also well-oiled.
6.  **Transfer and Shape:** Gently deflate the risen dough and carefully transfer it to the prepared baking pan. Pour another 2 tablespoons (30ml) of olive oil over the dough. With oiled hands, gently dimple and stretch the dough to fill the pan, being careful not to tear it. Don't worry if it springs back; you can let it rest for 10 minutes and then continue stretching.
7.  **Second Rise (Proofing):** Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let it rise again in a warm place for 45 minutes to 1 hour, or until visibly puffy.
8.  **Dimple and Top:** Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F) with a rack in the middle. Once the dough is puffy, uncover it. Drizzle the remaining 1 tablespoon (15ml) of olive oil generously over the surface. Use your fingertips to gently but firmly press deep dimples into the dough, almost touching the bottom of the pan. Sprinkle evenly with the chopped fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt.
9.  **Bake:** Place the focaccia in the preheated oven. Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until deeply golden brown on top and crispy on the bottom. The internal temperature should be around 90-95°C (195-205°F).
10. **Cool:** Once baked, immediately transfer the Focaccia Genovese from the pan to a wire rack to cool slightly. This prevents the bottom from becoming soggy.
11. **Serve:** Enjoy warm or at room temperature.

## Tips for Perfect Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia
Achieving that perfect balance of crispy crust and airy interior in your Focaccia Genovese is an art, but these tips will guide you:

*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** This cannot be stressed enough. A good quality extra virgin olive oil is the soul of Focaccia Genovese. It contributes significantly to flavor, aroma, and the desired texture. Don't skimp!
*   **High Hydration is Key:** The dough for authentic focaccia is very wet and sticky. This high water content is what creates the incredibly open, airy crumb. Resist the urge to add more flour during kneading, even if it feels challenging to work with.
*   **Gentle Handling:** Once the dough has risen, handle it very gently to avoid deflating the precious air bubbles that have formed. When stretching it into the pan, be patient and let the dough relax if it resists.
*   **Proper Proofing:** Don't rush the rising times. Both the bulk fermentation and the final proof are crucial for flavor development and achieving that light, airy texture. A warm, draft-free environment helps.
*   **Deep Dimples:** When dimpling, don't be shy! Press your fingertips firmly into the dough, almost to the bottom of the pan. These dimples are essential for holding the olive oil and salt, creating pockets of intense flavor.
*   **Hot Oven, Quick Bake:** Focaccia benefits from a hot oven (220°C / 425°F) to ensure a quick rise and a beautifully golden, crispy crust without drying out the interior.

## Serving Suggestions for Focaccia Genovese
The beauty of Focaccia Genovese lies in its versatility. While utterly delicious on its own, its delicate flavor and wonderful texture make it an excellent companion to a variety of dishes.

*   **Simply as a Snack:** The most traditional way to enjoy it is plain, perhaps with a morning coffee or as an afternoon pick-me-up.
*   **Antipasto Platter:** Serve alongside cured meats like prosciutto or salami, various cheeses (fresh mozzarella, provolone), marinated olives, and roasted vegetables for an authentic Italian spread.
*   **Dipping Delight:** Offer small bowls of high-quality extra virgin olive oil or balsamic glaze for dipping.
*   **Sandwich Bread:** Its sturdy yet tender nature makes it an ideal base for sandwiches. Fill it with pesto, sun-dried tomatoes, roasted red peppers, fresh mozzarella, and basil for a vegetarian delight, or with sliced turkey, ham, or mortadella.
*   **Soup and Salad Companion:** Tear off pieces to sop up rich broths from minestrone, lentil, or tomato soup, or serve alongside a crisp green salad with a light vinaigrette.
*   **Breakfast Treat:** A slice of Focaccia Genovese with a fried egg and a sprinkle of black pepper makes for a surprisingly satisfying breakfast.

## Focaccia Genovese Variations
While the classic Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia Genovese is perfect in its simplicity, the base dough is wonderfully adaptable to other toppings.

*   **Focaccia con le Cipolle (Onion Focaccia):** Sliced white or red onions, often caramelized slightly, are a very traditional topping in Liguria. Their sweetness beautifully complements the savory bread.
*   **Cherry Tomato:** Halved cherry tomatoes, pressed into the dimples before baking, add bursts of juicy, sweet-tart flavor and vibrant color.
*   **Olives:** Kalamata or Ligurian Taggiasca olives, pitted and scattered over the top, provide a briny, savory punch.
*   **Other Herbs:** While rosemary is classic, you can experiment with fresh thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs.
*   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of Parmesan, Pecorino, or even dollops of soft stracchino cheese can be added during the last few minutes of baking for a gooey, cheesy treat.
*   **Pesto:** A thin layer of traditional Genovese pesto spread over the focaccia before baking (or even after) is a regional favorite.
*   **Sweet Variations:** Though less common for Focaccia Genovese, some regions make sweet focaccia with grapes (focaccia all'uva) or sugar.

Focaccia Genovese is more than just a bread; it's an experience. Its humble ingredients, transformed by patient hands and a hot oven, yield a deeply satisfying and versatile food. The intoxicating aroma of rosemary and olive oil filling your kitchen, followed by the first bite of that airy, dimpled crust, is truly one of life's simple pleasures. Embrace the process, use the best ingredients you can find, and soon you'll be enjoying your own slice of Ligurian sunshine.
